title: Operators, Control Flow, and Defensive Branching
learningObjectives: Choose `if`/`else if` versus stacked `if` for exclusive rules
Design loops with provable termination and correct bounds
Implement FizzBuzz-style rules without duplicate outputs
Write trial-division primality checks for small integers

sections.title: Why operators and control flow decide production correctness
sections.content:
A payment batch job at 03:00 prints duplicate settlement lines because two independent `if` statements both fire for the same invoice when the amount is divisible by three and five. The developer assumed mutual exclusion without an `else if` ladder. The symptom is reconciled totals that disagree with the ledger by exactly the double-counted rows. The environment is a single-threaded JVM batch on Linux reading CSV inputs. The lifecycle moment is nightly settlement, not interactive traffic, which is why the bug hides until finance runs variance reports. Stakeholders only notice after auditors compare hash totals across subledgers, so the engineering team must prove branch exclusivity with logs and replay fixtures, not anecdotes.

Interviewers who ask about operators and control flow are testing whether you can narrate evaluation order, short-circuiting, and loop exit semantics under pressure. They are not checking whether you memorized precedence tables from a textbook. The gap between a junior answer and a staff answer is whether you connect `&&` and `||` to defensive coding against divide-by-zero and null-heavy expressions. You should be able to dry-run a five-line snippet on a whiteboard and state exactly which subexpressions execute. Strong candidates also mention how logging branch outcomes during incidents helps operators trust a hotfix without attaching a debugger to production traffic.

Use a four-part mental model for every branch. First, classify the expression as arithmetic, relational, logical, or assignment — and never confuse `=` with `==` in conditions. Second, decide whether short-circuit operators protect expensive or unsafe right-hand sides. Third, choose the right loop shape: definite counting with `for`, indefinite polling with `while`, or exactly-once-then-test with `do-while`. Fourth, name your exit strategy: `break` leaves the innermost loop or `switch`, `continue` skips to the next iteration, and `return` ends the whole method and is often the clearest guard style. When you document that model in pull requests, reviewers can verify invariants without reverse-engineering nested indentation.

At scale, nested loops in hot paths amplify tiny mistakes. An off-by-one on a retry cap turns into an infinite loop that pins a CPU until Kubernetes kills the pod. A missing `break` in a legacy `switch` duplicates events in an audit stream. A ternary nested three levels deep makes a pricing change impossible to review safely. Teams that skip code review on "simple" control flow pay with incident pages that cite obvious logic bugs because nobody traced the branches line by line. Observability alone cannot fix wrong predicates; metrics tell you that work is repeating, but only reading the loop guard explains why termination failed.

Senior engineers state invariants out loud: "this loop terminates because `i` strictly increases toward `n`" or "this guard guarantees `divisor` is non-zero before modulus." They prefer early `return` from small methods over deeply indented `else` blocks because flattening control flow reduces the chance that a future edit attaches logic to the wrong branch. They also know when `switch` on strings or enums improves readability and when pattern matching in modern Java replaces brittle integer codes. They treat every compound condition as a test candidate and insist on cases that flip each literal independently so mutation testing cannot sneak past CI.

In your daily job this topic appears in pull requests, design docs, and on-call. During review you ask whether mutually exclusive business rules use `else if` so only one action fires. During on-call you read a thread dump and recognize a tight loop that never increments its cursor because `continue` skipped the fix-up block. During sprint planning you push for table-driven tests that enumerate boundary days for billing windows. This day trains you to treat control flow as an observable contract, not clever syntax. When you write weekly retro notes, cite the branch or loop construct you fixed so junior engineers build a shared catalog of failure patterns instead of rediscovering FizzBuzz-style bugs quarterly.

sections.title: Theory — operators, branching, loops, and defensive evaluation
sections.content:
### Plain-language map of Java operators and control flow

Java programs compute values with **operators**, compare them with **relational** tests, combine predicates with **logical** operators, and steer execution with **if**, **loops**, **switch**, **break**, **continue**, and **return**. Most production defects in this area are not compiler errors; they are logic errors that pass type checking and only fail under specific combinations of inputs. **Interview angle:** Ask candidates to execute a short snippet mentally, naming every side effect, before you let them touch an editor—whiteboard discipline predicts on-call calm.

### Arithmetic and remainder semantics

`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, and `%` behave as expected on primitives with widening toward `int` or `long` when operands mix sizes. Integer division truncates toward zero, which surprises teams modeling ratios if they forget to cast to `double` explicitly. Remainder `%` preserves the sign of the dividend in Java, unlike some other languages—useful for cyclic indices but easy to misuse in calendar math. **Interview angle:** Pose `(7 / 3)` versus `(7.0 / 3)` and insist on exact types of intermediate results.

### Relational and equality on primitives versus references

Relational operators compare ordering for numeric types. `==` and `!=` on primitives compare values; on reference types they compare references unless you intentionally use `equals` for objects like `String`. Mixing `Integer` boxing with `==` is a classic interview trap unrelated to today's primitives focus but worth mentioning when discussing control-flow predicates that accidentally compare identities. **Interview angle:** Demand that the candidate separate **value** comparison from **reference** comparison whenever a predicate feeds a branch.

### Logical operators and short-circuit evaluation

`&&` and `||` short-circuit: for `&&`, if the left operand is false, the right operand is not evaluated; for `||`, if the left operand is true, the right is skipped. This is not a micro-optimization detail—it prevents `ArithmeticException` on divide-by-zero and avoids calling heavy methods when a cheap flag already decides the outcome. The bitwise variants `&` and `|` evaluate both sides and are rarely what you want for boolean control flow. **Interview angle:** Give `if (x != 0 && 10 / x > 1)` with `x = 0` and require an explanation of why no exception fires.

### Ternary operator and when to avoid it

The ternary `cond ? a : b` is an expression, so it can participate in assignments and returns. It is readable for simple choices but becomes a maintenance hazard when nested. Production style guides often limit nesting depth to one. **Interview angle:** Ask for a refactor from nested ternary to clear `if`/`return` guards—readability beats cleverness in services that live for years.

### if / else if / else ladders and mutual exclusion

Business rules that should fire **at most once** per iteration belong in a single ladder with `else if`. Independent `if` statements allow multiple bodies to execute, which is exactly the FizzBuzz defect at `i = 15`. Ordering matters: more specific predicates (divisible by both three and five) must precede partial predicates. **Interview angle:** Have the candidate fix a broken FizzBuzz that prints two lines for fifteen and explain the invariant "exactly one branch per `i`."

### switch, fall-through, and modern exhaustiveness

Traditional `switch` on integers, enums, or strings runs the selected case and falls through unless you `break`. Missing `break` duplicates behavior and shows up in event handlers. Newer `switch` expressions reduce this class of bugs by making each arm yield a value without accidental fall-through. **Interview angle:** Contrast accidental duplication in batch processing with intentional fall-through for grouped enum labels—only the latter belongs in modern code with explicit comments.

### for, while, do-while, and enhanced for

Indexed `for` loops expose the index for bounds work; enhanced `for` reads cleaner when you only consume elements. `while` fits unknown iteration counts with a clear guard; `do-while` guarantees one execution—handy for network read loops but dangerous if the guard can never become false. **Interview angle:** Ask how you would prove termination: monotone counter, decreasing work queue, or external event.

### break, continue, and labelled control transfer

`break` exits the innermost `switch` or loop; `continue` jumps to the next loop iteration. Labels allow breaking or continuing an outer loop—use sparingly and only when they clarify a search algorithm. Overusing `continue` can hide the fact that necessary cleanup never ran. **Interview angle:** Describe a bug where `continue` skipped a `finally`-like cleanup block that was mistakenly placed after the `continue`—symptom is resource leaks in long-lived loops.

### return as structured control flow

Early `return` after validating inputs flattens methods and avoids else-pyramids. In a loop inside a helper, `return` can exit as soon as a target is found—often clearer than a boolean flag. **Interview angle:** Compare `return` inside a loop versus setting a flag and breaking; discuss readability and testability.

### Operator precedence and parentheses policy

Precedence tables are easy to forget under stress. Production code typically parenthesizes non-trivial boolean expressions so the next reader does not need a spec. **Interview angle:** Show `a || b && c` and ask for explicit grouping—then mandate parentheses in real code reviews.

### Common numeric edge cases that steer branches

Comparisons with zero, negative lengths, and empty strings should appear in test matrices. Integer overflow does not throw; it wraps, which can flip a `>` comparison unexpectedly in financial counters. **Interview angle:** Tie overflow to choosing `long` or `BigDecimal` upstream—not a control-flow topic alone, but predicates lie if inputs already corrupted.

### Loop invariant checklist for reviewers

Before approving a loop, verify init, guard, progress, and postcondition. For nested loops, name complexity and ensure inner loop bounds shrink the problem. **Interview angle:** Ask for the Big-O of a nested scan and whether an early `break` changes average case behavior in a search.

### Production comparison table — branching constructs

| Construct | Best for | Primary risk |
|-----------|----------|--------------|
| if / else if | Mutually exclusive rules | Multiple `if` without `else` double-fires |
| switch | Discrete enum/string mapping | Missing `break` duplicates actions |
| for | Known iteration count | Off-by-one on bounds |
| while | Unknown count with guard | Non-terminating if guard never flips |
| return | Guard clauses in small methods | Hidden multiple exit points if abused |

### Short-circuit patterns in services

Use `if (list != null && !list.isEmpty())` so the emptiness check never runs on null. Use `if (cfg.isDryRun() || !paymentGateway.isUp())` to skip expensive calls when a cheap flag already decided. **Interview angle:** Ask what breaks if arguments are swapped—this reveals whether the engineer understands evaluation order.

### FizzBuzz and ordering as a template for feature flags

The FizzBuzz exercise is a stand-in for any rule engine where overlapping categories must resolve deterministically. Feature-flag combinations in billing often need a priority table rather than independent toggles. **Interview angle:** Map FizzBuzz ordering to "most specific wins" policies in configuration.

### Prime checks and trial division as loop practice

Primality for small `n` uses trial division up to `sqrt(n)` because any factor larger than the square root pairs with a smaller factor. Loops that skip even numbers after handling two are a micro-optimization lesson. **Interview angle:** Ask why checking divisors only up to `sqrt(n)` suffices—tests understanding of factor pairing.

### Sixty-second interview story

I treat **logical operators** as safety devices: **short-circuit** `&&` and `||` skip work that would throw or allocate. I express **mutually exclusive** business rules with **else if** so only one branch runs per value—this is the **FizzBuzz** lesson at fifteen. I pick **for** versus **while** based on whether the iteration count is known, and I use **early return** to keep validation readable. In **review** I trace **break** and **continue** to ensure cleanup still runs, and I reject nested **ternaries** that hide policy. On **on-call**, I correlate **CPU** hot loops with **non-terminating** guards. I verify fixes with **table-driven** tests at boundaries zero, one, negative, and max-ish values.

### Satyverse tie-down — trace before you type

Pick three snippets from this lesson, cover the screen, and narrate each branch outcome without running code. Repeat until the story matches execution. That habit converts syntax into reliable engineering judgment.

### Extra depth — assignment and incident bridges

Batch pipelines that classify rows with independent `if` statements mirror the broken FizzBuzz: two discounts or two fees can attach to one invoice when only one should win. **Interview angle:** Ask how you would encode priority—sorted rule list, `else if`, or strategy chain. Loop guards that compare scanner position to `length` without advancing on certain tokens mirror `continue` misuse: the index never reaches the end and the pod spins. **Interview angle:** Tie thread dumps showing the same stack frame repeating to a missing increment or a guard that never flips. Ternary-heavy pricing functions fail review because diffs cannot show which tier changed; flattening to table-driven rows makes Git history auditable for SOX-minded teams. **Interview angle:** Propose replacing nested ternary with a `record` row lookup. Short-circuiting is also a API design hint: optional parameters should be validated left-to-right so expensive deserialization never runs when a cheap feature flag is off. **Interview angle:** Discuss `@JsonIgnoreProperties` versus guard fields evaluated first in controller code. Enhanced `for` loops remove index mistakes but cannot replace scenarios where you need parallel arrays; mixing styles in one method is a readability smell unless comments justify it. **Interview angle:** Ask when you would refuse enhanced `for` for mutation-heavy transforms. Finally, document loop invariants beside non-trivial `while` loops so the next on-call engineer can prove termination in minutes instead of hours.

### Comparator mindset for interview follow-ups

| Situation | Prefer | Avoid |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Mutually exclusive promo codes | Ordered `else if` | Parallel `if` |
| Nullable collections before size | `!= null && !isEmpty()` | `!isEmpty()` alone |
| Unknown iterations with network I/O | `while` + timeout | Unbounded `do-while` |
| Search first match | `return` from helper | Deeply nested `break` flags |

### Numeric widening gotcha table

| Expression | Result type | Branching pitfall |
|------------|-------------|-------------------|
| `int / int` | `int` | Truncation skews threshold checks |
| `int * double` | `double` | Mixed-type compares need care |
| `%` with negatives | sign of dividend | Wrong cyclic index if sign flips |

### Incident archaeology patterns for control-flow regressions

When finance opens a ticket about doubled fees, the first diff to inspect is any refactor that replaced an else-if ladder with independent if statements that each enqueue work. When SRE pages about CPU saturation without traffic growth, the first search is while loops whose guards depend on state that continue statements skip updating. When support hears "sometimes the discount applies twice," the first hypothesis is fall-through in a switch that routes promotional codes. **Interview angle:** Ask the candidate to narrate how they bisect Git history between last known good deploy and failing deploy using binary search plus targeted integration replay. These incidents rarely need new frameworks; they need disciplined tracing of which branch executed for each input row. Capture representative rows in fixtures so the fix includes regression tests that print the chosen branch name for auditing. Pair metrics such as iteration counts with logging that toggles via feature flag so you can enable verbose branch tracing in staging without drowning production. Document the invariant that changed—mutual exclusivity, single side effect per message, termination within bounded steps—and store that text beside the code so the next refactor preserves intent.

### Teaching loops with finite state intuition

Every loop implements a tiny state machine: the index or cursor is state, the guard compares state to a goal, and the body plus increment are transitions. When you forget the transition, you trap the machine. Nested loops multiply states; the outer loop's progress must not depend on inner loops skipping via continue unless you prove the inner loop always advances the outer precondition. **Interview angle:** Ask for an invariant like "after each outer iteration, the scanned prefix is consistent" and verify it holds when continue fires inside the inner loop. Drawing the state on a whiteboard often exposes missing increments faster than staring at text. For data pipelines, tie the state to file offsets or sequence numbers so replay semantics stay obvious when operators reprocess a slice.

### Config flags, predicates, and ordering hazards

Modern services gate behavior with feature flags read from remote config. If you write `if (flagA) chargeFee(); if (flagB) waiveFee();` both may run. Prefer ordered evaluation tables or explicit precedence constants documented with the product owner. **Interview angle:** Discuss how LaunchDarkly-style percentage rollouts interact with branch ordering—partial exposure makes duplicate side effects visible only for some tenants. Testing requires matrices of flag combinations, not single happy paths. When migrating from boolean flags to enums, collapse overlapping booleans into one discriminant to make exclusivity syntactic instead of conventional.

### Static analysis and style guides that catch control-flow smells

Teams enable Checkstyle or SpotBugs rules that flag empty blocks, deeply nested if chains, and suspicious continue usage. Sonar equivalents highlight cognitive complexity scores tied to branching. None replace human review, but they keep obviously risky patterns out of mainline. **Interview angle:** Mention that complexity metrics justify extracting methods even when line count stays similar because named predicates document intent. Pair static analysis with mandatory integration tests for batch jobs where control flow encodes money movement.

### Cross-language parallels interviewers mention

Candidates from Python or JavaScript backgrounds may expect different truthiness rules; Java requires explicit boolean conditions, which reduces some surprises but not logic bugs. Kotlin and Scala introduce elvis operators and expression-oriented if that still demand clear exclusivity for business rules. **Interview angle:** Ask how Java's lack of truthy numbers changes guard style compared to scripting languages—forces explicit comparisons, which is good for payments code. Relate short-circuiting to optional chaining discussions without derailing into another language's semantics for long.

### Table — symptoms mapped to likely constructs

| Customer symptom | Likely construct | First code search |
|------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Duplicate ledger lines | Parallel if | Search paired postings same key |
| Stuck worker thread | while without progress | Inspect loop guard vs continue |
| Random double discounts | switch fall-through | Grep case blocks missing break |
| Flaky integration tests | Time-dependent if | Remove wall-clock; use injected clock |

### Closing tie-in to observability and SLO thinking

Service level objectives punish latency regressions caused by accidental quadratic loops where an early break disappeared during refactor. Error budgets also burn when duplicate side effects create compensating transactions that add load. **Interview angle:** Connect branch correctness to SRE practice: fewer surprises mean fewer emergency deploys and healthier error budgets. After fixing a control-flow bug, add a canary metric that compares counts of business events emitted per input row; drift immediately signals another exclusivity failure.

### Drill deck — rapid-fire patterns to internalize

Practice writing three versions of the same rule: nested if, else-if ladder, and table lookup via array or map for small domains. Vocalize short-circuit evaluation for five predicates involving null checks, empty strings, numeric bounds, feature flags, and IO readiness. **Interview angle:** Ask the candidate which representation they would pick for a rule set that changes weekly—table-driven code often beats recompilation cycles and keeps product managers able to read the matrix. Mention that overly clever bit-twiddling for booleans harms accessibility for new hires. Reinforce that Java forbids using integers directly as conditions, which pushes teams toward explicit comparisons that read well in diffs. End drills by timing how long it takes to spot a missing else-if in a twenty-line snippet; speed correlates with interview performance. Repeat weekly until the narration feels automatic under mild time pressure. Keep a short log of dry-run mistakes to measure improvement.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Chaining independent `if` statements for overlapping business rules — production symptom is duplicate charges or double log lines for one input; detect by diffing counts versus unique keys, fix with `else if` or explicit priority tables.

Placing expensive or throwing expressions on the right of `&&` without a null or zero guard — production symptom is sporadic `NullPointerException` or divide-by-zero in edge traffic; detect with guard logs, fix by reordering predicates with short-circuit-friendly left operands.

Using `continue` before mandatory cleanup in long loops — production symptom is connection pool exhaustion or file descriptor leaks; detect with pool metrics, fix by moving cleanup to `finally` or restructuring loop body.
Relying on `do-while` when the guard can stay false forever — production symptom is hung threads at 100% CPU; detect with thread dumps, fix by adding max iterations, timeouts, or provable progress.

Nesting ternary operators for pricing or tax logic — production symptom is wrong branch after a one-line edit during review fatigue; detect with mutation tests or table-driven specs, flatten to named booleans and `return`.

Forgetting `break` in classic `switch` — production symptom is fall-through causing duplicate side effects in event pipelines; detect with audit duplicates, add `break` or migrate to `switch` expressions.

Off-by-one loop bounds when translating inclusive business ranges — production symptom is skipped last day of billing or extra iteration hitting sentinel values; detect with property tests on boundaries, fix by aligning `<=` versus `<` with documented invariants.

Using `return` inside deeply nested loops without documenting early exit semantics — production symptom is missed finally-equivalent work in refactors; detect in code review checklists, fix by extracting search helpers with explicit Optional-style results.

sections.title: Exercise — Day 3 assignment bundle
sections.problem:
Implement one deterministic class for Day 3 assignment themes (operators, control flow, short-circuit, break/continue/return, FizzBuzz fix, primes).

Requirements:
1. Print a **short-circuit demo** line showing that `x != 0 && 10 / x > 1` evaluates safely when `x` is 0 (print the boolean result, no exception).
2. Print **three lines** from a small loop that uses **continue** for even indices and **break** after appending the third odd index value (explicit indices in output).
3. Print the **buggy** FizzBuzz-style output for `i = 15` using **two separate** `if` statements (two lines), then print the **fixed** single line using `else if` ordering.
4. Implement **isPrime(int n)** with trial division up to `sqrt(n)` without forbidden helpers, then print **all primes from 2 through 50** on one space-separated line.
5. End with one line stating that **mutually exclusive** rules belong in one **else-if** ladder.
hints: Keep output deterministic: never use randomness or wall-clock time in the solution.
For the short-circuit line, print the boolean result of the compound condition with x=0.
Reuse one `isPrime` implementation for both the exercise narrative and the 2..50 line.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is short-circuit evaluation for `&&` and `||` in Java?
conceptual.answer: Short-circuit evaluation means the right-hand operand of `&&` is not evaluated when the left is already false, and the right-hand operand of `||` is not evaluated when the left is already true. This preserves boolean logic while skipping work that would be redundant or unsafe. In services, engineers place cheap null checks or flag reads on the left and expensive calls or divisions on the right so failures short-circuit before side effects. Interviewers expect you to connect this to defensive coding, not just define the term. You should be able to predict whether `10 / x` runs for `x = 0` inside a compound condition. Teams encode these rules in style guides and unit tests so refactors cannot silently change evaluation order. During code review I ask for a dry-run narrative whenever a diff touches compound conditions or loop guards. That discipline transfers directly to on-call when you must decide whether a hot loop or duplicate posting is a logic bug rather than infrastructure noise.

followUps.question: How does **operators and control flow** surface during an incident review?
followUps.answer: I would cite the observable symptom first—duplicate rows, CPU peg, or skewed counters—then map it to the smallest control-flow construct involved, whether guard order, loop termination, or branch exclusivity. I would show the log line or metric that proved duplication or skew, paste the offending predicate order, and describe the code change such as reordering short-circuit operands or collapsing parallel if statements into one else-if ladder. I would close with the table-driven tests we added at boundaries zero, one, and combined divisors so finance or SRE stakeholders see deterministic proof before we close the ticket.

followUps.question: What follow-up trap do interviewers set on **operators and control flow**?
followUps.answer: They often permute logical AND operands or sneak a method call with side effects on the right-hand side to see whether you notice short-circuit evaluation rules. I answer by tracing evaluation order slowly, naming which subexpressions run, and stating why reordering fixes the bug without resorting to broad try or catch blocks. I also cite a review rule our team uses: never put IO or division on the right of AND unless a null or zero guard sits on the left, and I mention how we encode that in checklists for junior reviewers.

conceptual.question: How do `break`, `continue`, and `return` differ inside a loop?
conceptual.answer: `break` exits the innermost `switch` or loop immediately, `continue` skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the next test of the loop guard, and `return` exits the entire method carrying a value or void. In batch jobs, `break` often ends a search early, `continue` skips invalid records, and `return` ends helper methods that answer boolean questions about data. Confusing these produces subtle bugs such as skipped cleanup or premature method exit. Senior engineers narrate which construct they chose and why readability beats micro-optimizations. Teams encode these rules in style guides and unit tests so refactors cannot silently change evaluation order. During code review I ask for a dry-run narrative whenever a diff touches compound conditions or loop guards. That discipline transfers directly to on-call when you must decide whether a hot loop or duplicate posting is a logic bug rather than infrastructure noise.

I also cite a review rule our team uses: never put IO or division on the right of AND unless a null or zero guard sits on the left, and I mention how we encode that in checklists for junior reviewers.

conceptual.answer: Two independent `if` statements both evaluate true when `i` is divisible by three and five, so the first prints `Fizz` and the second prints `Buzz`. Mutually exclusive business rules need a single ladder—`else if`—or an explicit priority table. Production analogues include double billing when separate toggles each trigger. Fixing the ordering by testing divisibility by fifteen first encodes the real-world rule that the most specific condition wins. Tests should include fifteen, three, five, and primes. seniorScenario.question: Batch reprocessing job double-posts ledger lines after a feature flag change—how do you investigate?
seniorScenario.answer:
**Immediate response:** I stabilize blast radius first: pause risky rollouts, sample live failures, and confirm whether the issue is logical mis-branching, null-guard regression, or resource exhaustion secondary to retry loops. I collect `jcmd <pid> VM.version`, `jcmd <pid> VM.flags`, a short `jstack <pid>` for blocked paths, and request/error counters split by branch outcome to identify the failing decision edge in the first five minutes.

**Root cause:** For **Batch reprocessing job double-posts ledger lines after a feature flag change—how do you investigate?**, the usual root cause is mismatch between intended control logic and actual runtime behavior: operator precedence bugs, unsafe equality checks, non-short-circuited evaluation, or incomplete defensive branches around nullable/optional inputs. At JVM level this appears as branch bytecode executing a path that was considered impossible in code review, then cascading into retries, lock contention, or repeated exception creation.

**Fix:** I ship a narrow and testable correction: explicit parentheses for precedence, replacing wrapper/reference equality with value-safe comparisons, converting risky nested conditions into guard-clause flow, and adding deterministic fallback branches. I validate behavior with targeted tests plus `javap -c` bytecode spot checks and rollout canary metrics. If incident-driven, I add temporary feature-flag kill-switches and verify impact reduction through error-rate and latency SLO deltas.

**Prevention:** I institutionalize branch safety: review checklist for operator semantics, mandatory null/edge-case tests for every decision tree, static analysis rules for suspicious comparisons, and dashboard segmentation by branch category. I also capture Java-version notes (8/11/17/21) so migration plans include control-flow regression tests, not just compile success, and on-call runbooks include concrete diagnostic commands for branch-related incidents.

followUps.question: How do you communicate branch fixes to stakeholders?
followUps.answer: I summarize customer-visible impact, reconciliation variance, and SLA burn before naming the faulty construct, whether parallel if statements or a non-terminating while loop. I give a confidence level for the fix, link the pull request diff that shows the else-if ladder or progress guard, and attach test output or metrics screenshots so finance and leadership see proof without reading bytecode. I close with any residual risks such as replay jobs still running on old artifacts.

followUps.question: Which metric proves the loop fix worked?
followUps.answer: CPU seconds per batch file, iterations per record, and wall-clock duration per million rows should return to the pre-regression baseline within one deploy. I also watch duplicate counters in audit streams when the bug was double-fired branches, and I set alerts on max loop depth or zero-progress detectors so the next regression pages on-call immediately instead of burning CPU silently overnight.

wrongAnswers: `&&` always evaluates both sides — short-circuit skips the right when the left decides the outcome; reorder guards to exploit this safely.
`break` exits every nested loop — `break` only leaves the innermost loop unless a label targets an outer loop.
`continue` exits the loop — `continue` skips to the next iteration; `break` or `return` exits.
Multiple `if` statements are equivalent to `else if` — independent `if` can fire together; `else if` enforces exclusivity.
Integer division rounds to nearest — truncation toward zero breaks naive thresholds; cast or use decimal types when needed.
`return` inside a loop only skips one iteration — `return` ends the entire method immediately.
Ternary operators cannot be nested — they can, but deep nesting is discouraged for readability.
`switch` on String compares references with `==` internally — `switch` uses `equals` semantics for strings; still prefer `equals` manually outside switch.

jobSwitch.resumeBullet: Reduced branch-related production defects 42% by redesigning control-flow guards and enforcing operator-safety checks across APIs.
jobSwitch.interviewPositioning: I treat operators and branching as reliability architecture. In my first week, I map high-risk decision trees, add guard-clause standards, and validate with branch-level metrics plus bytecode spot checks using javap.
jobSwitch.starAnchor: Situation: A payment API had repeated incidents from inconsistent null and precedence handling in eligibility logic. Task: Cut branch-related outages without blocking feature delivery. Action: I refactored nested decisions into guarded predicates, added truth-table tests for edge cases, and rolled out branch observability dashboards tied to error spikes. Result: branch-regression incidents dropped by 42% in one quarter and MTTR improved from 52 to 18 minutes.

sections.title: MCQ Drill - Operators, Control Flow, and Defensive Branching

questions.question: What does Java **short-circuit** mean for `&&`?

options.B: Right side executes only if left side is true

questions.explanation: `&&` stops evaluation when left side is false, so right side executes only when needed. B is correct. A is wrong because that behavior matches `&` on booleans, not `&&`; C is wrong because left side is always evaluated first; D is wrong because at least the left expression is evaluated. In production this matters because misunderstanding this can trigger unintended null dereferences in condition chains.

questions.question: Which operator compares **object references** instead of values?
questions.explanation: `==` checks identity for objects, not semantic value equality. B is correct. A is wrong because `equals` is value-based when implemented correctly; C is wrong because `compareTo` is ordering, not identity comparison; D is wrong because `Objects.equals` safely delegates to value equality. In production this matters because using identity checks in business rules creates intermittent branch bugs.

Integer division truncates toward zero. A is correct. B is wrong because fractional output requires floating-point operands; C is wrong because rounding up does not occur in integer division; D is wrong because the code is valid. In production this matters because integer truncation in rate formulas can silently undercount allocations and quotas.

Expression evaluates left to right: `x++` gives 2 then `++x` gives 4, total 6. B is correct. A is wrong because ignores pre-increment side effect; C is wrong because would require different evaluation order; D is wrong because Java defines this expression legally. In production this matters because complex increment expressions reduce readability and cause costly logic regressions.

questions.question: When should you prefer a **guard clause** over deep nested `if` blocks?

options.A: Never, nested if is always clearer
options.B: When rejecting invalid states early improves flow and safety

questions.explanation: Guard clauses flatten control flow and reduce branch complexity. B is correct. A is wrong because deep nesting is often harder to reason about; C is wrong because guard clauses are for production logic too; D is wrong because they apply broadly, not just loops. In production this matters because flattened defensive branching reduces missed null/edge conditions in hot services.

questions.question: A null check exists but `NullPointerException` still occurs in a condition. Most likely cause?

options.B: Used `&` instead of `&&` so right side still evaluated

questions.explanation: `&` on booleans evaluates both operands, so null access can still happen. B is correct. A is wrong because `&&` is the safer short-circuit variant; C is wrong because JIT does not break language semantics; D is wrong because compiler preserves conditional logic. In production this matters because wrong operator choice can spike runtime exceptions and alert fatigue.

options.B: `LOW`

questions.explanation: Ternary chooses first branch when condition is true. A is correct. B is wrong because condition is true so second branch is not selected; C is wrong because expression returns string branches, not boolean; D is wrong because statement is valid. In production this matters because ternary misuse in config resolution can silently pick wrong environment settings.

questions.question: What is true about `switch` on strings (Java 7+)?

options.B: Supported and compiled into hash/equals branch logic

questions.explanation: String switch is compiled into efficient branch checks based on hash and equals. B is correct. A is wrong because string switch has long been supported; C is wrong because feature predates modern LTS releases; D is wrong because no reflection is required. In production this matters because ignoring switch behavior can lead to inaccurate assumptions in hot-path dispatch.

questions.question: Why can `if (a == b)` be dangerous for `Integer` values above 127?

options.B: Reference caching range is limited, identity check may fail

questions.explanation: Integer caching is limited, so identity comparisons become value-dependent bugs. B is correct. A is wrong because Java allows it but semantics are often wrong; C is wrong because auto-unboxing does not always happen here; D is wrong because identity is deterministic, not random. In production this matters because authorization or pricing branches can diverge unpredictably by input value.

Both conditions are true; x ends at 3 and prints 3. B is correct. A is wrong because misses second increment; C is wrong because no additional increment occurs; D is wrong because condition evaluates true so output occurs. In production this matters because side effects inside conditions increase defect risk during urgent fixes.

questions.question: What bytecode instruction family represents integer conditional branches?

options.B: `if_icmp*`

questions.explanation: `if_icmp*` instructions encode integer comparison branch behavior. B is correct. A is wrong because array allocation is unrelated to comparisons; C is wrong because method invocation does not itself encode branch condition; D is wrong because type cast checks are separate from branch compare instructions. In production this matters because bytecode-level understanding helps diagnose branch miscompilation assumptions.

options.B: `false`

questions.explanation: `||` short-circuits, so `s.length()` is not evaluated when first part is true. A is correct. B is wrong because first condition is true; C is wrong because short-circuit prevents right-side evaluation; D is wrong because code is valid. In production this matters because proper short-circuiting avoids avoidable exception storms.

questions.question: Incident: CPU spikes after adding complex nested `if-else` decision tree. Best first refactor?

options.B: Replace nested tree with guard clauses and extracted predicates

questions.explanation: Guard clauses and named predicates reduce cognitive and runtime branch complexity. B is correct. A is wrong because heap tuning does not fix branch logic design; C is wrong because removing logs hides symptoms; D is wrong because business decisioning still needs safe backend control flow. In production this matters because unmanaged branch complexity slows incidents and increases regression rate.

questions.question: When should `switch` be preferred over long `if-else` chains?

options.B: When branching on one variable against many discrete constants

questions.explanation: Switch improves readability and often compiles to efficient jump logic. B is correct. A is wrong because there are clear, common cases where switch is superior; C is wrong because booleans are not typical switch use; D is wrong because production dispatch logic is a primary use case. In production this matters because incorrect branch form increases maintenance cost and onboarding time.

options.B: `false`

questions.explanation: `&&` has higher precedence than `||`, so first conjunction is true. A is correct. B is wrong because given p=5, main conjunction is true; C is wrong because expression is valid; D is wrong because operator precedence is language-defined. In production this matters because precedence misunderstandings cause silent branch inversions in critical logic.

questions.question: What is a strong defensive branching practice for external inputs?

options.B: Normalize early and branch on validated invariants only

questions.explanation: Normalization + invariant checks reduce error-path ambiguity. B is correct. A is wrong because untrusted input must be validated; C is wrong because silent catches hide branch failure modes; D is wrong because random fallbacks create nondeterministic behavior. In production this matters because defensive invariants reduce production bugs from malformed payloads.

questions.question: A migration from Java 8 to 17 changed string handling in condition checks. What should be audited first?

options.B: Implicit charset and locale-dependent comparisons

questions.explanation: Version shifts in defaults can alter branch outcomes for parsing/normalization paths. B is correct. A is wrong because heap size is secondary to logical correctness here; C is wrong because thread priority is unrelated to string-comparison semantics; D is wrong because index tuning does not fix control-flow correctness. In production this matters because missed default changes can trigger branch regressions post-upgrade.

options.B: `BC`

questions.explanation: Without `break`, switch falls through from case 3 into default. B is correct. A is wrong because ignores fall-through behavior; C is wrong because case 1 does not match; D is wrong because fall-through is legal. In production this matters because missing breaks can trigger unintended policy execution paths.

questions.question: On-call sees sudden 4xx spikes due to branch that rejects optional fields. Immediate robust change?

options.B: Introduce explicit null/blank guards with feature-flagged rollout

questions.explanation: Targeted defensive guards with controlled rollout reduce user impact safely. B is correct. A is wrong because removing validation opens correctness/security issues; C is wrong because retries amplify bad-branch load; D is wrong because capacity does not fix branch semantics. In production this matters because defensive guard strategy shortens MTTR while preserving data integrity.

questions.question: Which command best helps inspect compiled branch structure of a class?

options.B: `jmap -histo <pid>`

questions.explanation: `javap -c` disassembles bytecode and shows branch instructions directly. A is correct. B is wrong because heap histogram does not reveal branch bytecode; C is wrong because heap info is memory-oriented; D is wrong because thread dump shows stacks, not compiled instruction flow. In production this matters because bytecode visibility is key when source intent and runtime flow diverge.

questions.question: A high-traffic API has branch explosion from 14 nested conditions. Architect-level first step?

options.A: Add more nested branches for edge cases
options.B: Model decisions as explicit rules with deterministic evaluation order
options.D: Inline all checks into one expression

questions.explanation: Rule modeling with explicit ordering makes behavior auditable and testable. B is correct. A is wrong because more nesting worsens complexity; C is wrong because removing tests increases rollout risk; D is wrong because mega-expressions are harder to review and debug. In production this matters because structured rule engines reduce outage risk from branching regressions.

questions.question: Latency regression appears only after JDK upgrade. Branch-heavy code unchanged. What do you verify first?

options.B: JIT warmup/compilation behavior and branch hot spots under load

questions.explanation: Runtime/JIT behavior can shift across versions even with identical source. B is correct. A is wrong because network-only focus misses CPU-side branch effects; C is wrong because removing checks trades correctness for temporary speed; D is wrong because permanent downgrade ignores security and maintenance realities. In production this matters because version-aware profiling prevents false root-cause assumptions.

questions.question: Fraud pipeline incorrectly approves risky transactions due to operator precedence bug. Best preventive control?

options.A: Rely on manual code review comments only
options.B: Enforce parentheses and branch truth-table tests in CI

questions.explanation: Truth-table tests and explicit grouping catch precedence regressions early. B is correct. A is wrong because manual review alone is inconsistent; C is wrong because heap size is unrelated to logical correctness; D is wrong because suppressed alerts delay detection. In production this matters because strong branch testing reduces high-severity business logic incidents.

questions.question: A critical branch depends on floating-point equality. Production misroutes occur. Staff fix?

options.B: Use tolerance/range-based comparison and domain-safe types

questions.explanation: Floating-point branch decisions need tolerance or deterministic numeric modeling. B is correct. A is wrong because exact equality is brittle for binary floating-point; C is wrong because string comparison is semantically unsafe for numeric decisions; D is wrong because random thresholds violate deterministic behavior. In production this matters because incorrect numeric branching can route traffic to wrong risk or pricing paths.

questions.question: Canary shows error spikes only on one branch after feature flag rollout. Best incident pattern?

options.B: Toggle feature flag off for affected cohort, capture branch metrics, patch narrowly

questions.explanation: Scoped rollback with evidence collection balances stability and learning. B is correct. A is wrong because global rollback may be excessive and expensive; C is wrong because ignoring canary defeats safety controls; D is wrong because restarts hide but do not fix faulty branching. In production this matters because controlled flag operations reduce blast radius and preserve deployment velocity.

questions.question: Branch logic for role checks is duplicated across services and diverges. Architect response?

options.B: Centralize authorization decision library with contract tests

questions.explanation: Shared decision contracts prevent semantic drift in distributed systems. B is correct. A is wrong because divergence causes policy inconsistencies; C is wrong because manual sync is error-prone; D is wrong because backend enforcement is still mandatory. In production this matters because centralized branch policy reduces compliance and security regressions.

questions.question: A code review finds `if (obj != null & obj.isReady())`. What should happen?

options.B: Reject and replace with `&&`, then add regression test

questions.explanation: `&` forces right-side evaluation and can trigger null dereference. B is correct. A is wrong because operators are not equivalent in evaluation semantics; C is wrong because this can become production-impacting quickly; D is wrong because exceptions are not a substitute for correct branching. In production this matters because operator misuse in guard paths creates preventable runtime exceptions.

questions.question: Decision tree performance is inconsistent across environments. Which evidence is most useful?

options.B: JFR/async-profiler traces plus identical workload branch distribution

questions.explanation: Profiling with matched workload reveals real branch hotspots and JIT effects. B is correct. A is wrong because local-only data is rarely representative; C is wrong because opinions are not runtime evidence; D is wrong because docs cannot substitute measurement. In production this matters because evidence-driven tuning avoids chasing non-existent bottlenecks.

questions.question: A migration introduces Java switch expressions, but fallback behavior changes. Staff action?

options.B: Add exhaustive branch tests for legacy vs new behavior and explicit default handling

questions.explanation: Behavioral parity tests ensure refactor safety beyond compile correctness. B is correct. A is wrong because old tests may not cover new expression paths; C is wrong because blanket bans lose useful language improvements; D is wrong because compile success does not prove semantic parity. In production this matters because branch parity tests prevent silent policy drift after language upgrades.

questions.question: Postmortem shows most outages stem from branch edge cases. What long-term control is strongest?

options.B: Decision-table design reviews, mutation tests, and branch observability dashboards

questions.explanation: Systematic branch governance combines design quality, verification, and runtime visibility. B is correct. A is wrong because retries can amplify logical faults; C is wrong because capacity masks symptoms without fixing branch correctness; D is wrong because manual-only testing misses many edge combinations. In production this matters because institutional controls reduce repeat incidents and improve release confidence.

sections.title: Operators and control flow — interview cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Topic | Rule of thumb | Interview one-liner |
|-------|---------------|---------------------|
| Short-circuit && | Put cheap safe checks on the left | "I order guards so the RHS never runs when it would throw." |
| Short-circuit || | Skip heavy work when a flag already passes | "I rely on || to bypass expensive checks during bypass mode." |
| else if ladder | One outcome per evaluation | "Mutually exclusive rules belong in one ladder, not stacked ifs." |
| break | Leaves innermost loop/switch | "break stops the search; labels are rare and justified." |
| continue | Next iteration | "continue skips the rest of this pass—watch cleanup placement." |
| return | Ends method | "Early return documents invalid states before happy path logic." |
| for vs while | Known vs unknown trip count | "I pick for for counted work and while for open-ended polling." |
| do-while | At least one body run | "do-while is for probes, but I still prove termination." |
| FizzBuzz lesson | Most specific divisor first | "I test 15 before 3 or 5 to keep output single-valued." |
| Trial division | Stop at sqrt(n) | "Factors pair across sqrt; inner loop proves primality." |
| Ternary | One level max | "If it needs nesting, I refactor to named booleans." |
| De Morgan | Flip negated AND/OR | "I simplify !(a&&b) to !a||!b for readable guards." |
| switch fall-through | Explicit breaks | "Accidental fall-through duplicates production side effects." |
| Off-by-one | Match inclusive/exclusive spec | "I test zero, one, and max lengths on every loop." |
| Guard clauses | Fail fast | "I return on bad input so reviewers see invariants early." |
